channel.title: PowerShell on RaihnForge
channel.description: Recent content in PowerShell on RaihnForge

item.title: Narya
item.description:
Narya - the Ring of Fire is a tiny Windows system-tray tool that answers a question laptops keep hidden: which apps are actually using my real GPU, and how hard?

Modern laptops ship with two graphics chips - a power-hungry discrete GPU (NVIDIA / AMD / Intel Arc) and an integrated one baked into the CPU. Windows quietly decides, per app, which one runs. That decision is invisible, and “is the expensive GPU even being used?” is a question you can usually only answer by digging through Task Manager. Narya makes it an ambient glance.

item.title: Malware Analysis: Reading Obfuscated PowerShell Payloads
item.description: The assignment provided three captured command samples that looked like legitimate Windows operations on the surface and turned out to be malware payloads underneath. The exercise asked four questions of each sample: what is it trying to do, is it malicious, is it reaching out to another URL or IP, what other files is it trying to download, and how could a hacker make these commands harder to spot. The walkthrough below is the reasoning I worked out for each sample.
